intro: Law in the biblical sense encompasses several distinct categories. The law of nature is the moral will of God discoverable through creation and conscience (Rom. 1:20; 2:14–15). The Mosaic law (Hebrew Torah) is the covenant legislation given at Sinai, comprising ceremonial, civil, and moral dimensions, of which the Ten Commandments are the foundational summary (Ex. 20; Deut. 5). Crucially, the law was given to a redeemed people — the exodus preceded Sinai — as the terms of covenant life, not as a means of obtaining salvation. Paul contrasts law and grace (Rom. 6:14–15) and argues that the law served as a custodian to lead Israel to Christ (Gal. 3:24). The moral law, summarized by Jesus as love of God and neighbor (Matt. 22:37–40), remains the standard of righteousness, fulfilled by Christ and written on the hearts of believers by the Spirit (Jer. 31:33; Rom. 8:4).
